Question:
A buffer receives log events with bursty arrivals. The mean arrival rate is below service rate, but bursts overflow the buffer unless batching is enabled. Which systems explanation is strongest? Synthetic v2 id 1.

Choices:
A. If average arrival rate is lower, overflow is mathematically impossible.
B. Mean-rate stability is not enough; burst variance and finite capacity require smoothing or backpressure.
C. Batching helps only by deleting events before they enter the queue.
D. Finite buffers behave like infinite queues whenever utilization is below one.
bad_response: <think>This selects a plausible distractor before checking the constraint.</think><answer>C</answer>
failure_reason: Chooses the nearest single-factor explanation instead of the two-stage causal account.

Question:

Question:
A heat pump report says the compressor work input stayed fixed while the useful heat delivered rose after insulation was added. Which interpretation best separates energy conservation from coefficient-of-performance improvement? Synthetic v2 id 45.

Choices:
A. Energy conservation is violated because delivered heat became larger than work input.
B. Energy is conserved; insulation reduces losses, so more delivered heat per unit work improves COP.
C. The compressor must have secretly consumed less work because COP cannot change otherwise.
D. The reservoir temperature is irrelevant, so insulation cannot affect delivered heat.
bad_response: <think>This selects a plausible distractor before checking the constraint.</think><answer>C</answer>

Question:
A buffer receives log events with bursty arrivals. The mean arrival rate is below service rate, but bursts overflow the buffer unless batching is enabled. Which systems explanation is strongest? Synthetic v2 id 46.

Choices:
A. If average arrival rate is lower, overflow is mathematically impossible.
B. Batching helps only by deleting events before they enter the queue.
C. Mean-rate stability is not enough; burst variance and finite capacity require smoothing or backpressure.
D. Finite buffers behave like infinite queues whenever utilization is below one.

Question:
A chemical sensor has high sensitivity but moderate specificity. In a low-prevalence setting, many positive alerts are false. Which conclusion follows from conditional probability? Synthetic v2 id 89.

Choices:
A. High sensitivity alone guarantees that most positive alerts are true positives.
B. Low prevalence can make the positive predictive value modest even when sensitivity is high.
C. Specificity affects only negative alerts and not the positive predictive value.
D. Prevalence is irrelevant after a positive test result has already occurred.

Question:

B. The compressor must have secretly consumed less work because COP cannot change otherwise.
C. Energy is conserved; insulation reduces losses, so more delivered heat per unit work improves COP.
D. The reservoir temperature is irrelevant, so insulation cannot affect delivered heat.

Question:

B. Batching helps only by deleting events before they enter the queue.

Question:
A buffer receives log events with bursty arrivals. The mean arrival rate is below service rate, but bursts overflow the buffer unless batching is enabled. Which systems explanation is strongest? Synthetic v2 id 133.

Choices:
A. If average arrival rate is lower, overflow is mathematically impossible.
B. Mean-rate stability is not enough; burst variance and finite capacity require smoothing or backpressure.
C. Batching helps only by deleting events before they enter the queue.
D. Finite buffers behave like infinite queues whenever utilization is below one.
bad_response: <think>This selects a plausible distractor before checking the constraint.</think><answer>C</answer>
failure_reason: Chooses the nearest single-factor explanation instead of the two-stage causal account.

Question:
A heat pump report says the compressor work input stayed fixed while the useful heat delivered rose after insulation was added. Which interpretation best separates energy conservation from coefficient-of-performance improvement? Synthetic v2 id 177.

Choices:
A. Energy conservation is violated because delivered heat became larger than work input.
B. Energy is conserved; insulation reduces losses, so more delivered heat per unit work improves COP.
C. The compressor must have secretly consumed less work because COP cannot change otherwise.
D. The reservoir temperature is irrelevant, so insulation cannot affect delivered heat.
bad_response: <think>This selects a plausible distractor before checking the constraint.</think><answer>C</answer>
failure_reason: Chooses the nearest single-factor explanation instead of the two-stage causal account.

Question:
A buffer receives log events with bursty arrivals. The mean arrival rate is below service rate, but bursts overflow the buffer unless batching is enabled. Which systems explanation is strongest? Synthetic v2 id 178.

Choices:
A. If average arrival rate is lower, overflow is mathematically impossible.
B. Batching helps only by deleting events before they enter the queue.
C. Mean-rate stability is not enough; burst variance and finite capacity require smoothing or backpressure.
D. Finite buffers behave like infinite queues whenever utilization is below one.

Question:
A chemical sensor has high sensitivity but moderate specificity. In a low-prevalence setting, many positive alerts are false. Which conclusion follows from conditional probability? Synthetic v2 id 221.

Choices:
A. High sensitivity alone guarantees that most positive alerts are true positives.
B. Low prevalence can make the positive predictive value modest even when sensitivity is high.
C. Specificity affects only negative alerts and not the positive predictive value.
D. Prevalence is irrelevant after a positive test result has already occurred.

Question:
A heat pump report says the compressor work input stayed fixed while the useful heat delivered rose after insulation was added. Which interpretation best separates energy conservation from coefficient-of-performance improvement? Synthetic v2 id 222.

Choices:

B. The compressor must have secretly consumed less work because COP cannot change otherwise.
C. Energy is conserved; insulation reduces losses, so more delivered heat per unit work improves COP.
D. The reservoir temperature is irrelevant, so insulation cannot affect delivered heat.

Question:

B. Batching helps only by deleting events before they enter the queue.

D. Mean-rate stability is not enough; burst variance and finite capacity require smoothing or backpressure.

Question:
A buffer receives log events with bursty arrivals. The mean arrival rate is below service rate, but bursts overflow the buffer unless batching is enabled. Which systems explanation is strongest? Synthetic v2 id 265.

Choices:
A. If average arrival rate is lower, overflow is mathematically impossible.
B. Mean-rate stability is not enough; burst variance and finite capacity require smoothing or backpressure.
C. Batching helps only by deleting events before they enter the queue.
D. Finite buffers behave like infinite queues whenever utilization is below one.
bad_response: <think>This selects a plausible distractor before checking the constraint.</think><answer>C</answer>
failure_reason: Chooses the nearest single-factor explanation instead of the two-stage causal account.

Question:
A heat pump report says the compressor work input stayed fixed while the useful heat delivered rose after insulation was added. Which interpretation best separates energy conservation from coefficient-of-performance improvement? Synthetic v2 id 309.

Choices:
A. Energy conservation is violated because delivered heat became larger than work input.
B. Energy is conserved; insulation reduces losses, so more delivered heat per unit work improves COP.
C. The compressor must have secretly consumed less work because COP cannot change otherwise.
D. The reservoir temperature is irrelevant, so insulation cannot affect delivered heat.
bad_response: <think>This selects a plausible distractor before checking the constraint.</think><answer>C</answer>
failure_reason: Chooses the nearest single-factor explanation instead of the two-stage causal account.

Question:
A buffer receives log events with bursty arrivals. The mean arrival rate is below service rate, but bursts overflow the buffer unless batching is enabled. Which systems explanation is strongest? Synthetic v2 id 310.

Choices:
A. If average arrival rate is lower, overflow is mathematically impossible.
B. Batching helps only by deleting events before they enter the queue.
C. Mean-rate stability is not enough; burst variance and finite capacity require smoothing or backpressure.
D. Finite buffers behave like infinite queues whenever utilization is below one.

Question:
A chemical sensor has high sensitivity but moderate specificity. In a low-prevalence setting, many positive alerts are false. Which conclusion follows from conditional probability? Synthetic v2 id 353.

Choices:
A. High sensitivity alone guarantees that most positive alerts are true positives.
B. Low prevalence can make the positive predictive value modest even when sensitivity is high.
C. Specificity affects only negative alerts and not the positive predictive value.
D. Prevalence is irrelevant after a positive test result has already occurred.

Question:

B. The compressor must have secretly consumed less work because COP cannot change otherwise.
C. Energy is conserved; insulation reduces losses, so more delivered heat per unit work improves COP.
D. The reservoir temperature is irrelevant, so insulation cannot affect delivered heat.

B. Batching helps only by deleting events before they enter the queue.

Question:
A buffer receives log events with bursty arrivals. The mean arrival rate is below service rate, but bursts overflow the buffer unless batching is enabled. Which systems explanation is strongest? Synthetic v2 id 397.

Choices:
A. If average arrival rate is lower, overflow is mathematically impossible.
B. Mean-rate stability is not enough; burst variance and finite capacity require smoothing or backpressure.
C. Batching helps only by deleting events before they enter the queue.
D. Finite buffers behave like infinite queues whenever utilization is below one.
bad_response: <think>This selects a plausible distractor before checking the constraint.</think><answer>C</answer>
failure_reason: Chooses the nearest single-factor explanation instead of the two-stage causal account.

Question:
A heat pump report says the compressor work input stayed fixed while the useful heat delivered rose after insulation was added. Which interpretation best separates energy conservation from coefficient-of-performance improvement? Synthetic v2 id 441.

Choices:
A. Energy conservation is violated because delivered heat became larger than work input.
B. Energy is conserved; insulation reduces losses, so more delivered heat per unit work improves COP.
C. The compressor must have secretly consumed less work because COP cannot change otherwise.
D. The reservoir temperature is irrelevant, so insulation cannot affect delivered heat.
bad_response: <think>This selects a plausible distractor before checking the constraint.</think><answer>C</answer>
failure_reason: Chooses the nearest single-factor explanation instead of the two-stage causal account.

Question:
A buffer receives log events with bursty arrivals. The mean arrival rate is below service rate, but bursts overflow the buffer unless batching is enabled. Which systems explanation is strongest? Synthetic v2 id 442.

Choices:
A. If average arrival rate is lower, overflow is mathematically impossible.
B. Batching helps only by deleting events before they enter the queue.
C. Mean-rate stability is not enough; burst variance and finite capacity require smoothing or backpressure.
D. Finite buffers behave like infinite queues whenever utilization is below one.

Question:
A chemical sensor has high sensitivity but moderate specificity. In a low-prevalence setting, many positive alerts are false. Which conclusion follows from conditional probability? Synthetic v2 id 485.

Choices:
A. High sensitivity alone guarantees that most positive alerts are true positives.
B. Low prevalence can make the positive predictive value modest even when sensitivity is high.
C. Specificity affects only negative alerts and not the positive predictive value.
D. Prevalence is irrelevant after a positive test result has already occurred.

Question:
A heat pump report says the compressor work input stayed fixed while the useful heat delivered rose after insulation was added. Which interpretation best separates energy conservation from coefficient-of-performance improvement? Synthetic v2 id 486.

B. The compressor must have secretly consumed less work because COP cannot change otherwise.
C. Energy is conserved; insulation reduces losses, so more delivered heat per unit work improves COP.
D. The reservoir temperature is irrelevant, so insulation cannot affect delivered heat.

Question:

B. Batching helps only by deleting events before they enter the queue.

D. Mean-rate stability is not enough; burst variance and finite capacity require smoothing or backpressure.

Question:
A buffer receives log events with bursty arrivals. The mean arrival rate is below service rate, but bursts overflow the buffer unless batching is enabled. Which systems explanation is strongest? Synthetic v2 id 529.

Choices:
A. If average arrival rate is lower, overflow is mathematically impossible.
B. Mean-rate stability is not enough; burst variance and finite capacity require smoothing or backpressure.
C. Batching helps only by deleting events before they enter the queue.
D. Finite buffers behave like infinite queues whenever utilization is below one.
bad_response: <think>This selects a plausible distractor before checking the constraint.</think><answer>C</answer>
failure_reason: Chooses the nearest single-factor explanation instead of the two-stage causal account.

Question:

Question:
A heat pump report says the compressor work input stayed fixed while the useful heat delivered rose after insulation was added. Which interpretation best separates energy conservation from coefficient-of-performance improvement? Synthetic v2 id 573.

Choices:
A. Energy conservation is violated because delivered heat became larger than work input.
B. Energy is conserved; insulation reduces losses, so more delivered heat per unit work improves COP.
C. The compressor must have secretly consumed less work because COP cannot change otherwise.
D. The reservoir temperature is irrelevant, so insulation cannot affect delivered heat.
bad_response: <think>This selects a plausible distractor before checking the constraint.</think><answer>C</answer>
failure_reason: Chooses the nearest single-factor explanation instead of the two-stage causal account.

Question:
A buffer receives log events with bursty arrivals. The mean arrival rate is below service rate, but bursts overflow the buffer unless batching is enabled. Which systems explanation is strongest? Synthetic v2 id 574.

Choices:
A. If average arrival rate is lower, overflow is mathematically impossible.
B. Batching helps only by deleting events before they enter the queue.
C. Mean-rate stability is not enough; burst variance and finite capacity require smoothing or backpressure.
D. Finite buffers behave like infinite queues whenever utilization is below one.
bad_response: <think>This selects a plausible distractor before checking the constraint.</think><answer>D</answer>

Question:
A chemical sensor has high sensitivity but moderate specificity. In a low-prevalence setting, many positive alerts are false. Which conclusion follows from conditional probability? Synthetic v2 id 617.

Choices:
A. High sensitivity alone guarantees that most positive alerts are true positives.
B. Low prevalence can make the positive predictive value modest even when sensitivity is high.
C. Specificity affects only negative alerts and not the positive predictive value.
D. Prevalence is irrelevant after a positive test result has already occurred.

Question:

B. The compressor must have secretly consumed less work because COP cannot change otherwise.
C. Energy is conserved; insulation reduces losses, so more delivered heat per unit work improves COP.
D. The reservoir temperature is irrelevant, so insulation cannot affect delivered heat.

B. Batching helps only by deleting events before they enter the queue.

Question:
A heat pump report says the compressor work input stayed fixed while the useful heat delivered rose after insulation was added. Which interpretation best separates energy conservation from coefficient-of-performance improvement? Synthetic v2 id 879.

Choices:
A. Energy conservation is violated because delivered heat became larger than work input.
B. The compressor must have secretly consumed less work because COP cannot change otherwise.
C. The reservoir temperature is irrelevant, so insulation cannot affect delivered heat.
D. Energy is conserved; insulation reduces losses, so more delivered heat per unit work improves COP.
